data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The simultaneous collapse resulted from a synchronized climatic shock (the 3.2ka event) causing prolonged drought that specifically devastated the rain-fed agricultural hinterlands of the Northern Levant and Anatolia, leading to mass migration and famine that overwhelmed the static palatial storage systems, while Egypt survived due to its reliance on predictable Nile flooding.", "mechanism": "Exogenous climatic forcing causing agricultural failure and subsequent demographic pressure/migration.", "scope": {"covers": ["Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states", "Mycenaean Greece"], "excludes": ["Egypt", "Mesopotamia"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Paleoclimatic data indicating normal precipitation levels in the Eastern Mediterranean between 1200-1150 BC.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "A high-typicality environmental determinist explanation focusing on the specific vulnerability of rain-fed vs. irrigation-based agriculture."}
